title: Distances, circles & spheres
summary: From the general definition $d(X,Y)=\min d(P,Q)$, every distance (point-line, point-plane, line-line, plane-plane) reduces to a point-to-point measurement along a perpendicular. We master the point-plane formula and the skew-line cross-product formula, then meet circles and spheres: completing the square to get center and radius, the radical axis/plane to intersect two of them, the relation $r^2=R^2-d(C,\pi)^2$ for the circle a plane cuts on a sphere, and the discriminant/distance tests for tangency and intersection of lines and planes with these quadratic objects.
objectives: Compute distances point-point, point-line, point-plane, line-plane, line-line (parallel and skew), and plane-plane by reducing each to a perpendicular point-to-point distance.
Apply the point-plane formula $|ax_P+by_P+cz_P+d|/\sqrt{a^2+b^2+c^2}$ and the skew-line cross-product formula correctly, including when each one fails.
Read off the center and radius of a circle or sphere by completing the square, and detect degenerate (point / empty) cases.
Find the intersection of two circles or two spheres via the radical axis / radical plane and classify it (empty, tangent point, or circle).
Compute the center and radius of the circle a plane cuts on a sphere using $r^2=R^2-d(C,\pi)^2$.
Decide tangency vs. secant vs. disjoint for a line/plane with a circle/sphere using the discriminant or the distance-vs-radius test.
Construct a circle/sphere tangent at a given point, or passing through given points, by solving the appropriate linear conditions.

blocks.text: Distances, circles & spheres

This topic ties together everything we know about points, lines and planes by asking a single geometric question: **how far apart are two objects?** The general definition is $$d(X,Y)=\min\{d(P,Q): P\in X,\ Q\in Y\}.$$ If the two objects meet, the distance is $0$. The art is to turn each case (point-line, point-plane, line-line, plane-plane) into a *point-to-point* distance using **orthogonality**: we drop a perpendicular and measure along it. We then meet two new objects defined by distance — the **circle** in $\mathbb{R}^2$ and the **sphere** in $\mathbb{R}^3$ — and learn to read off their center and radius by **completing the square**, and to decide tangency/intersection.

**The master idea.** Every distance reduces to a point-to-point distance along a perpendicular. For a circle/sphere, distance is always measured from the **center** and then corrected by the **radius** $R$.
Point–plane distance (the workhorse formula)

Let $\pi:ax+by+cz+d=0$ with normal $\mathbf{n}=(a,b,c)$, and let $P=(x_P,y_P,z_P)$. Then $$d(P,\pi)=\frac{|a\,x_P+b\,y_P+c\,z_P+d|}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2+c^2}}.$$ Geometrically: take the line $r$ through $P$ with $r\perp\pi$, let $Q=\pi\cap r$; then $d(P,\pi)=d(P,Q)$.

blocks.title: Point to plane
blocks.text: Distance from $Q(1,2,3)$ to $\pi:x+y+z=0$. Here $\mathbf{n}=(1,1,1)$, $|\mathbf{n}|=\sqrt3$, $d=0$. $$d(Q,\pi)=\frac{|1+2+3|}{\sqrt{3}}=\frac{6}{\sqrt3}=2\sqrt3.$$ (The same plane in 2D gives the line formula: from $Q(1,2)$ to $r:x+y=0$ we get $\frac{|1+2|}{\sqrt2}=\frac{3\sqrt2}{2}$.)

**Normalize before you trust $|\,\mathbf p\cdot\mathbf n-d\,|$.** The clean form "distance $=|\mathbf p\cdot\mathbf n-d|$" only holds when $|\mathbf n|=1$. If the normal is not a unit vector you MUST divide by $\sqrt{a^2+b^2+c^2}$ — forgetting the denominator is the single most common error here.
Point–line and line–line distance

steps.text: Find the plane $\alpha$ through $P$ perpendicular to $r$: its normal is the direction vector $\mathbf v_r$, so $\alpha:\ \mathbf v_r\cdot(\mathbf x-P)=0$.
Intersect: $Q=\alpha\cap r$ (plug the parametric line into $\alpha$, solve for $t$).

blocks.text: If lines $r,s$ **intersect**, $d=0$. If they are **parallel**, $d(r,s)=d(P,s)$ for any $P\in r$ (reduce to point-line). If they are **skew** (or crossing), with directions $\mathbf v_r,\mathbf v_s$ and points $P\in r$, $Q\in s$: $$d(r,s)=\frac{|\,\overrightarrow{PQ}\cdot(\mathbf v_r\times \mathbf v_s)\,|}{\|\mathbf v_r\times \mathbf v_s\|}.$$

**The cross-product formula breaks for parallel lines.** If $\mathbf v_r\parallel \mathbf v_s$ then $\mathbf v_r\times\mathbf v_s=\mathbf 0$ and you divide by zero. For parallel lines always reduce to a point-line distance instead. Also: if the numerator $\overrightarrow{PQ}\cdot(\mathbf v_r\times\mathbf v_s)=0$ the lines are **coplanar** (they intersect), so $d=0$.

blocks.title: Point to a line in space
blocks.text: $Q(1,2,3)$, line $r:\,x+y+z=0,\ y+z=0$. Parametrize: from $y+z=0$ get $z=-y$; then $x+y+z=x=0$. So $r:(x,y,z)=(0,t,-t)$, direction $\mathbf v_r=(0,1,-1)$. Plane through $Q$ perpendicular to $r$: $0(x-1)+1(y-2)-1(z-3)=0\Rightarrow y-z+1=0$. On $r$: $t-(-t)+1=0\Rightarrow t=-\tfrac12$, so $Q_0=(0,-\tfrac12,\tfrac12)$. Then $d(Q,r)=\sqrt{1+(2+\tfrac12)^2+(3-\tfrac12)^2}=\frac{3\sqrt6}{2}.$
Circles: center & radius by completing the square

A circle in $\mathbb{R}^2$ is the solution set of $$x^2+y^2+Dx+Ey+F=0.$$ Completing the square gives $(x-x_c)^2+(y-y_c)^2=R^2$ with center $C(-\tfrac{D}{2},-\tfrac{E}{2})$ and $R^2=\tfrac{D^2}{4}+\tfrac{E^2}{4}-F$. If $R^2=0$ the "circle" is a single point; if $R^2<0$ it is empty (imaginary).

question.prompt: What are the center and radius of $x^2+y^2+x+y=1$?
question.explanation: Here $D=E=1$, $F=-1$. Center $=(-\tfrac{D}{2},-\tfrac{E}{2})=(-\tfrac12,-\tfrac12)$. $R^2=\tfrac14+\tfrac14-(-1)=\tfrac32$, so $R=\sqrt{3/2}=\tfrac{\sqrt6}{2}$. B has the wrong center sign and a wrong radius; C completes the square incorrectly; D uses the right center but reports $R=\tfrac32$ ($R^2=9/4$), not $3/2$.

blocks.text: Spheres and the radical plane

A sphere is $x^2+y^2+z^2+Dx+Ey+Fz+G=0$, i.e. $d(P,C)=R$ with $$C\!\left(-\tfrac{D}{2},-\tfrac{E}{2},-\tfrac{F}{2}\right),\quad R=\sqrt{\tfrac{D^2}{4}+\tfrac{E^2}{4}+\tfrac{F^2}{4}-G}.$$ Found by completing the square in all three variables (same recipe as the circle, one more term).

**Intersecting two spheres / two circles.** Subtract the two equations: the quadratic terms $x^2+y^2(+z^2)$ cancel, leaving a **linear** equation — the **radical axis** (line, for circles) or **radical plane** (for spheres). Crucially $$S_1\cap S_2=S_1\cap\pi=S_2\cap\pi,$$ so intersecting two spheres becomes intersecting one sphere with a plane — a problem we can solve.

blocks.title: Sphere ∩ plane (the circle in space): center & radius

steps.text: Get the sphere's center $C$ and radius $R$ by completing the square.
Compare: if $h>R$ → empty; if $h=R$ → one point (tangent); if $h<R$ → a circle.

For the circle: its **center** is the foot of the perpendicular from $C$ onto $\pi$ (intersect $\pi$ with the line through $C$ along $\mathbf n$), and its **radius** is $r=\sqrt{R^2-h^2}$ (Pythagoras).

blocks.tex: r^2=R^2-d(C,\pi)^2\qquad(\text{radius of the circle cut on a sphere by a plane})

blocks.title: Circle in space
blocks.text: $C:\ x^2+y^2+z^2=4,\ \ x+y+z=1$. Sphere center $O(0,0,0)$, $R=2$. The circle's center is $\pi\cap\ell$ where $\ell$ is the line through $O$ along $\mathbf n=(1,1,1)$, i.e. $x=y=z$; plugging into $x+y+z=1$ gives the center $C'(\tfrac13,\tfrac13,\tfrac13)$. Then $d(O,\pi)=\frac{|0+0+0-1|}{\sqrt3}=\tfrac{1}{\sqrt3}$, so $r^2=4-\tfrac13=\tfrac{11}{3}$.
Tangency and intersection with spheres/circles

To intersect a **line/plane** with a circle/sphere: substitute the line/plane into the quadratic, getting a quadratic in one variable with discriminant $\Delta$. Then $\Delta>0$ → two points (secant), $\Delta=0$ → one point (**tangent**), $\Delta<0$ → empty. Equivalently, compare $d(C,\text{object})$ with $R$: tangent $\iff d(C,\text{line})=R$.

**Position of a point/line/circle relative to a circle** (center $C$, radius $R$):
- Point $P$: $d(P,\mathcal C)=|d(P,C)-R|$.
- Line $\ell$: $d(\ell,\mathcal C)=\max(0,\ d(\ell,C)-R)$.
- Two circles $\mathcal C_1,\mathcal C$: they intersect $\iff |R_1-R|\le d(C_1,C)\le R_1+R$. If $d(C_1,C)<|R_1-R|$ one circle is **inside** the other.

question.prompt: For which condition is the line $\ell$ tangent to the circle $\mathcal C$ of center $C$, radius $R$?
question.explanation: Tangency means the line touches the circle at exactly one point, i.e. the distance from the center to the line equals the radius: $d(C,\ell)=R$ (equivalently $\Delta=0$ when you substitute). A means the line passes through the center (a diameter, secant). B is the secant case (two intersection points). D means the line misses the circle entirely (empty intersection).

blocks.text: Building a tangent circle/sphere & passing through points

**Tangent circle at a given point $P$ to a line $s$.** The center lies on the line $t$ through $P$ perpendicular to $s$ (the normal direction). Pick any center $C$ on $t$ (other than $P$), set $R=d(C,P)$, and write $(x-x_C)^2+(y-y_C)^2=R^2$. The same logic builds a tangent sphere to a plane: center on the normal line through $P$, radius $=d(C,P)$.

**Through given points.** A circle through 3 non-collinear points (or a sphere through 4 non-coplanar points) is found by imposing $x_i^2+y_i^2+Dx_i+Ey_i+F=0$ for each point — a **linear** system in $D,E,F$ (and $G$).

blocks.title: Circle tangent to a line at a point
blocks.text: Circle tangent to $s:x+y=0$ at $P(0,0)$. Slope of $s$ is $-1$, so the perpendicular $t$ has slope $1$: $t:y=x$, centers $C(a,a)$. Take $C(2,2)$: $R=d(C,P)=\sqrt{4+4}=2\sqrt2$. The circle is $(x-2)^2+(y-2)^2=8$, i.e. $x^2+y^2-4x-4y=0$. (Many answers exist — any $C\ne P$ on $t$ works.)

**Sanity checks that catch arithmetic slips.** (1) The center coordinates are *minus half* the linear coefficients — sign errors are common. (2) After completing the square, verify $R^2\ge 0$; a negative value means you made a sign mistake or the object is genuinely empty. (3) For sphere∩plane, you must have $d(C,\pi)\le R$ or there's no circle.
